Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is the primary purpose of a computer network?
options: To share hardware, software, and data resources among connected devices
To increase the processing speed of individual computers
To create backup copies of files automatically
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: To share hardware, software, and data resources among connected devices

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: Which network type covers a geographic area such as a city or metropolitan region?
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: MAN

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What does bandwidth refer to in networking?
options: The physical width of a cable
The maximum data transfer rate of a network connection
The number of devices connected to a network
The distance a signal can travel
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: The maximum data transfer rate of a network connection

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: How many layers does the OSI model have?
Which OSI layer is responsible for end-to-end communication and reliable data transfer?
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Transport Layer

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is the function of the Physical Layer in the OSI model?
options: Transmitting raw bits over a physical medium
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Transmitting raw bits over a physical medium

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: Which transmission medium offers the highest bandwidth and is immune to electromagnetic interference?
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Fiber optic cable

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is latency in networking?
options: The amount of data transferred per second
The time delay between sending and receiving data
The number of hops between source and destination
The error rate of a connection
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: The time delay between sending and receiving data

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What does TCP/IP model's 'Internet Layer' correspond to in the OSI model?
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Network Layer

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is the difference between circuit switching and packet switching?
options: Circuit switching is for wireless; packet switching is for wired
Circuit switching reserves dedicated path; packet switching routes data as independent packets
Packet switching is slower but more reliable than circuit switching
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Circuit switching reserves dedicated path; packet switching routes data as independent packets

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: Which layer of OSI model is responsible for data encryption and compression?
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Presentation Layer

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is throughput in networking?
options: Actual data rate achieved in practice
The number of packets lost per second
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Actual data rate achieved in practice

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is propagation delay?
options: Time to transmit a packet from source to medium
Time for a signal to travel from sender to receiver across the medium
Time to process a packet at a router
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Time for a signal to travel from sender to receiver across the medium

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: In the OSI model, which layer handles logical addressing (IP addresses)?
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Network Layer

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is the role of the Session Layer in the OSI model?
options: Establishes, manages, and terminates sessions between applications
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Establishes, manages, and terminates sessions between applications

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is the function of the Data Link Layer?
options: Reliable node-to-node data transfer and framing
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Reliable node-to-node data transfer and framing

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: Which layer of the TCP/IP model combines the OSI's Physical and Data Link layers?
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Network Access Layer

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is the unit of data at the Network Layer called?
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Packet

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What does 'store and forward' switching mean?
options: The switch stores the entire frame before forwarding it
Packets are stored permanently in routing tables
Data is stored in cloud before forwarding
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: The switch stores the entire frame before forwarding it

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is the maximum data rate of Fast Ethernet?
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: 100 Mbps

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is the PDU (Protocol Data Unit) at the Data Link Layer?
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Frame

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is the difference between a hub and a switch?
options: A hub sends data to all ports; a switch sends data to the specific destination port
A switch broadcasts to all; a hub is targeted
A hub operates at Layer 3; a switch at Layer 2
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: A hub sends data to all ports; a switch sends data to the specific destination port

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What does the term 'encapsulation' mean in networking?
options: Wrapping data with protocol headers as it moves down the OSI layers
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Wrapping data with protocol headers as it moves down the OSI layers

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is a protocol in networking?
options: A set of rules governing how devices communicate
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: A set of rules governing how devices communicate

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is the difference between UTP and STP cables?
options: UTP has no shielding; STP has a metallic shield to reduce interference
STP is for wireless; UTP is for wired
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: UTP has no shielding; STP has a metallic shield to reduce interference

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: Which network metric measures the percentage of time a network is available?
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Availability (uptime)

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is jitter in networking?
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Variation in packet delay (inconsistent latency)

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is the difference between the OSI model and TCP/IP model?
options: OSI has 7 layers and is theoretical; TCP/IP has 4 layers and is practical implementation
TCP/IP has 7 layers; OSI has 4 layers
Both have the same number of layers
OSI is used for the internet; TCP/IP is academic only
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: OSI has 7 layers and is theoretical; TCP/IP has 4 layers and is practical implementation

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: What is a collision domain?
options: An area where network devices are physically located
A network segment where packets can collide during simultaneous transmission
A protected zone in a firewall
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: A network segment where packets can collide during simultaneous transmission

Network Introduction and OSI Model.question: Which device operates at the Network Layer (Layer 3) of the OSI model?
Network Introduction and OSI Model.answer: Router

Application Layer.question: What does HTTP stand for and what is its primary use?
options: HyperText Transfer Protocol – for transferring web pages over the internet
High Transfer Text Protocol – for secure data transfer
Hyperlink Transmission Transfer Protocol – for linking websites
HyperText Transport Program – for hosting web servers
Application Layer.answer: HyperText Transfer Protocol – for transferring web pages over the internet

Application Layer.question: What is the default port number for HTTP?
What does DNS stand for and what does it do?
options: Dynamic Network Service – assigns IP addresses dynamically
Domain Name System – translates domain names to IP addresses
Data Network Standard – standardizes data formats
Distributed Name Server – distributes server load
Application Layer.answer: Domain Name System – translates domain names to IP addresses

Application Layer.question: What is the difference between HTTP and HTTPS?
options: HTTPS uses SSL/TLS encryption; HTTP transmits data in plaintext
HTTP is for web pages; HTTPS is for file downloads
Application Layer.answer: HTTPS uses SSL/TLS encryption; HTTP transmits data in plaintext

Application Layer.question: What is an HTTP GET request used for?
options: Sending large amounts of data to a server
Application Layer.answer: Retrieving data/resources from a server

Application Layer.question: Which HTTP method is used to send form data to a server?
Application Layer.answer: POST

Application Layer.question: What is the purpose of DNS caching?
options: Storing web pages for offline access
Storing resolved domain-to-IP mappings to avoid repeated DNS lookups
Application Layer.answer: Storing resolved domain-to-IP mappings to avoid repeated DNS lookups

Application Layer.question: What port does SMTP use for email sending?
What is the difference between SMTP, POP3, and IMAP?
options: SMTP sends emails; POP3 and IMAP receive emails (IMAP keeps mail on server)
All three are for sending emails
IMAP is for sending; SMTP and POP3 are for receiving
POP3 keeps mail on server; IMAP downloads and deletes from server
Application Layer.answer: SMTP sends emails; POP3 and IMAP receive emails (IMAP keeps mail on server)

Application Layer.question: What is a DNS A record?
options: Maps domain name to IPv4 address
Maps domain name to mail server
Maps domain name to IPv6 address
Maps alias domain to canonical domain name
Application Layer.answer: Maps domain name to IPv4 address

Application Layer.question: What is socket programming?
options: Network programming using socket API to establish connections between client and server
A technique for multiplexing network connections
Application Layer.answer: Network programming using socket API to establish connections between client and server

Application Layer.question: What is an HTTP cookie?
options: Small data stored on client browser to maintain session state
Application Layer.answer: Small data stored on client browser to maintain session state

Application Layer.question: What is the difference between a web server and a web client?
options: Web server stores and serves content; web client requests and displays it
Web client stores content; web server requests it
Both serve and request content equally
Web server only handles HTTPS; web client handles HTTP
Application Layer.answer: Web server stores and serves content; web client requests and displays it

Application Layer.question: What is HTTP persistent connection (HTTP/1.1)?
options: A connection that transfers unlimited data
A single TCP connection reused for multiple HTTP requests
Application Layer.answer: A single TCP connection reused for multiple HTTP requests

Application Layer.question: What is an IP address and what does it uniquely identify?
options: A logical address uniquely identifying a device on a network
Application Layer.answer: A logical address uniquely identifying a device on a network

Application Layer.question: What is the role of a port number in networking?
options: Identifies the specific application or service on a host
Application Layer.answer: Identifies the specific application or service on a host

Application Layer.question: What is a DNS MX record?
options: Maps domain name to IPv4 address
Specifies the mail server responsible for receiving email for a domain
Application Layer.answer: Specifies the mail server responsible for receiving email for a domain

Application Layer.question: What HTTP status code indicates 'Not Found'?
What is a well-known port number?
options: Ports 0-1023 reserved for standard services by IANA
Ports used for secure communications only
Application Layer.answer: Ports 0-1023 reserved for standard services by IANA

Application Layer.question: In client-server socket programming, which side calls accept() to wait for connections?
Application Layer.answer: Server

Application Layer.question: What is the purpose of HTTP caching?
options: Storing copies of responses to reduce server load and improve performance
Application Layer.answer: Storing copies of responses to reduce server load and improve performance

Application Layer.question: What is the FTP protocol used for?
options: Transferring files between client and server
Application Layer.answer: Transferring files between client and server

Application Layer.question: What does a DNS resolver do?
options: Queries DNS servers on behalf of clients to resolve domain names to IPs
Routes DNS queries to the correct network
Application Layer.answer: Queries DNS servers on behalf of clients to resolve domain names to IPs

Application Layer.question: What is the difference between HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2?
options: HTTP/2 uses binary framing and multiplexing for improved performance
HTTP/1.1 is faster due to simpler design
HTTP/2 only works with HTTP (not HTTPS)
Application Layer.answer: HTTP/2 uses binary framing and multiplexing for improved performance

Application Layer.question: What port does HTTPS use?
What is an authoritative DNS server?
options: A server that caches DNS responses
The server that holds the actual DNS records for a domain and provides definitive answers
A server that forwards DNS queries
Application Layer.answer: The server that holds the actual DNS records for a domain and provides definitive answers

Application Layer.question: What does the HTTP 200 OK status code mean?
options: The request was successful and the response contains the requested data
Application Layer.answer: The request was successful and the response contains the requested data

Application Layer.question: In socket programming, what does bind() do?
options: Associates the socket with a specific IP address and port number
Application Layer.answer: Associates the socket with a specific IP address and port number

Application Layer.question: What is a hierarchical DNS namespace?
options: DNS organized in a tree structure with root, TLD, and authoritative servers
Application Layer.answer: DNS organized in a tree structure with root, TLD, and authoritative servers

Application Layer.question: What is the purpose of the User-Agent header in HTTP?
options: Identifies the client software (browser, OS) making the request
Application Layer.answer: Identifies the client software (browser, OS) making the request

Transport Layer.question: What is the main difference between TCP and UDP?
options: TCP is faster; UDP is more reliable
TCP is connection-oriented and reliable; UDP is connectionless and best-effort
UDP uses more bandwidth than TCP
TCP is for video streaming; UDP is for web browsing
Transport Layer.answer: TCP is connection-oriented and reliable; UDP is connectionless and best-effort

Transport Layer.question: What is the TCP three-way handshake?
options: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK sequence used to establish a TCP connection
DATA → ACK → FIN sequence for data transfer
CONNECT → CONFIRM → START used for any protocol
SYN → ACK → SYN used for UDP connections
Transport Layer.answer: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK sequence used to establish a TCP connection

Transport Layer.question: What is multiplexing in the Transport Layer?
options: Combining multiple signals on one physical channel
Multiple application processes using the network simultaneously via port numbers
Splitting a data stream into multiple paths
Transport Layer.answer: Multiple application processes using the network simultaneously via port numbers

Transport Layer.question: What is flow control in TCP?
options: Preventing the sender from overwhelming the receiver by using sliding window
Controlling the number of active TCP connections
Transport Layer.answer: Preventing the sender from overwhelming the receiver by using sliding window

Transport Layer.question: What is congestion control in TCP?
options: Preventing traffic jams on physical roads
Reducing transmission rate when network congestion is detected to avoid packet loss
Controlling the number of users on a network
Transport Layer.answer: Reducing transmission rate when network congestion is detected to avoid packet loss

Transport Layer.question: What is demultiplexing in the Transport Layer?
options: Delivering received segments to the correct application using destination port numbers
Transport Layer.answer: Delivering received segments to the correct application using destination port numbers

Transport Layer.question: Why is UDP preferred over TCP for video streaming?
options: UDP's lower overhead and no retransmission delays are acceptable for real-time media
UDP is more secure than TCP
UDP supports higher bitrates than TCP
Transport Layer.answer: UDP's lower overhead and no retransmission delays are acceptable for real-time media

Transport Layer.question: What does the TCP sliding window control?
options: The number of packets that can be in transit (unacknowledged) at any time
The physical window size of the TCP header
The number of TCP connections allowed simultaneously
Transport Layer.answer: The number of packets that can be in transit (unacknowledged) at any time

Transport Layer.question: What is a TCP segment?
options: The unit of data at the Transport Layer
A fragment of a packet at Network Layer
A frame at Data Link Layer
A bit sequence at Physical Layer
Transport Layer.answer: The unit of data at the Transport Layer

Transport Layer.question: What happens during TCP connection termination (four-way handshake)?
options: FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK sequence allows both sides to close the connection
RST packet immediately terminates the connection from both sides
Two FIN packets are exchanged simultaneously
Transport Layer.answer: FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK sequence allows both sides to close the connection

Transport Layer.question: What is TCP slow start?
options: TCP starting with a large window then reducing it
TCP beginning with a small congestion window that doubles with each successful round-trip until threshold
A congestion avoidance technique using AIMD
TCP reducing speed when packets arrive slowly
Transport Layer.answer: TCP beginning with a small congestion window that doubles with each successful round-trip until threshold

Transport Layer.question: What port does UDP use for DNS queries?
What is the purpose of TCP acknowledgment (ACK)?
options: To confirm receipt of data and indicate the next expected sequence number
To request retransmission of lost packets only
Transport Layer.answer: To confirm receipt of data and indicate the next expected sequence number

Transport Layer.question: What is TCP retransmission?
options: Resending segments when no acknowledgment is received within timeout period
Retransmitting corrupted frames at Data Link Layer
Transport Layer.answer: Resending segments when no acknowledgment is received within timeout period

Transport Layer.question: What is the checksum field in TCP/UDP headers used for?
options: Error detection by verifying data integrity during transmission
Transport Layer.answer: Error detection by verifying data integrity during transmission

Transport Layer.question: What does it mean that TCP is 'full-duplex'?
options: TCP can only send in one direction at a time
Both sides can send and receive data simultaneously
Transport Layer.answer: Both sides can send and receive data simultaneously

Transport Layer.question: What is the difference between TCP congestion control's slow start and congestion avoidance phases?
options: Slow start doubles cwnd per RTT; congestion avoidance increases linearly by 1 MSS per RTT
Congestion avoidance is faster than slow start
Slow start is used for UDP; congestion avoidance for TCP
Transport Layer.answer: Slow start doubles cwnd per RTT; congestion avoidance increases linearly by 1 MSS per RTT

Transport Layer.question: What is a well-known port used by UDP for DHCP client?
What does the sequence number in a TCP segment represent?
options: The total number of segments sent
The byte number of the first byte of data in the segment
The segment's position in a batch
Transport Layer.answer: The byte number of the first byte of data in the segment

Transport Layer.question: What is the purpose of the TIME_WAIT state in TCP?
options: Waiting for the server to respond
Waiting to ensure the remote end received the final ACK before closing
Transport Layer.answer: Waiting to ensure the remote end received the final ACK before closing

Transport Layer.question: What is AIMD in TCP congestion control?
options: Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease – increases window linearly, halves on congestion
Adaptive Internet Multiplexing Design – a routing protocol
Transport Layer.answer: Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease – increases window linearly, halves on congestion

Transport Layer.question: For which applications is UDP typically preferred over TCP?
options: DNS queries, live video streaming, and online gaming
Transport Layer.answer: DNS queries, live video streaming, and online gaming

Transport Layer.question: What is the maximum segment size (MSS) in TCP?
options: Maximum size of an IP packet
Maximum amount of data in a TCP segment (typically 1460 bytes)
Maximum number of segments in a window
Maximum size of a TCP header
Transport Layer.answer: Maximum amount of data in a TCP segment (typically 1460 bytes)

Transport Layer.question: What is the purpose of TCP urgent pointer?
options: To indicate out-of-band urgent data that should be processed immediately
To set packet priority in QoS
Transport Layer.answer: To indicate out-of-band urgent data that should be processed immediately

Transport Layer.question: What is the UDP header size?
Transport Layer.answer: 8 bytes

Transport Layer.question: How does TCP ensure reliable delivery?
options: Through sequence numbers, acknowledgments, and retransmissions
Transport Layer.answer: Through sequence numbers, acknowledgments, and retransmissions

Transport Layer.question: What is TCP fast retransmit?
options: Retransmitting a lost segment upon receiving 3 duplicate ACKs without waiting for timeout
Sending TCP segments at maximum speed
Transport Layer.answer: Retransmitting a lost segment upon receiving 3 duplicate ACKs without waiting for timeout

Transport Layer.question: What does a socket consist of in networking?
Transport Layer.answer: IP address + Port number combination

Transport Layer.question: What is the main advantage of using UDP for DNS queries?
options: DNS requires the reliability of TCP
UDP's low overhead is suitable for small, single-request/response DNS queries
DNS uses both UDP and TCP equally
UDP is more secure for DNS lookups
Transport Layer.answer: UDP's low overhead is suitable for small, single-request/response DNS queries

Transport Layer.question: What happens to a TCP connection when a RST (reset) segment is received?
options: The connection is immediately and forcefully terminated
Transport Layer.answer: The connection is immediately and forcefully terminated

Network Layer.question: What is the primary function of the Network Layer?
options: Logical addressing and routing packets from source to destination
Network Layer.answer: Logical addressing and routing packets from source to destination

Network Layer.question: What is an IP address?
options: A physical hardware address burned into a NIC
A logical 32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6) address identifying a device on a network
A port number for application identification
Network Layer.answer: A logical 32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6) address identifying a device on a network

Network Layer.question: What does subnetting allow network administrators to do?
options: Increase the total number of IP addresses
Divide a large network into smaller subnetworks for efficient address use
Network Layer.answer: Divide a large network into smaller subnetworks for efficient address use

Network Layer.question: What is CIDR notation and what does /24 mean?
options: Classless Inter-Domain Routing; /24 means 24 bits for network, 8 bits for hosts
Class-based IP Domain Routing; /24 means 24 host addresses
Centralized IP Dual Routing; /24 means 24 routers
Compact IP Data Record; /24 is a 24-byte record
Network Layer.answer: Classless Inter-Domain Routing; /24 means 24 bits for network, 8 bits for hosts

Network Layer.question: How many usable host addresses does a /24 subnet provide?
What is distance vector routing?
options: Each router knows the complete network topology
Each router shares its routing table with neighbors; routes determined by hop count or cost
Network Layer.answer: Each router shares its routing table with neighbors; routes determined by hop count or cost

Network Layer.question: What is link state routing?
options: Routing based only on link speed
Each router floods topology info; all routers build complete map and run Dijkstra's algorithm
Distance vector sharing between all routers
Network Layer.answer: Each router floods topology info; all routers build complete map and run Dijkstra's algorithm

Network Layer.question: What is the routing protocol RIP based on?
options: Distance vector algorithm with hop count as metric
Network Layer.answer: Distance vector algorithm with hop count as metric

Network Layer.question: What is the maximum hop count in RIP and what does it signify?
options: 15 hops; networks beyond 15 hops are considered unreachable (infinity)
16 hops; the network is too large
Network Layer.answer: 15 hops; networks beyond 15 hops are considered unreachable (infinity)

Network Layer.question: What is the difference between connection-oriented and connectionless networks?
options: Connection-oriented (like virtual circuits) sets up a path before data transfer; connectionless (like IP) routes each packet independently
Both have identical data delivery mechanisms
Network Layer.answer: Connection-oriented (like virtual circuits) sets up a path before data transfer; connectionless (like IP) routes each packet independently

Network Layer.question: What does TTL (Time to Live) do in an IP packet?
options: Sets packet expiration time in seconds
Decremented at each router; packet discarded when TTL reaches 0 to prevent infinite loops
Specifies how long DNS cache is valid
Sets session timeout for TCP connections
Network Layer.answer: Decremented at each router; packet discarded when TTL reaches 0 to prevent infinite loops

Network Layer.question: What is NAT (Network Address Translation)?
options: Converting domain names to IP addresses
Mapping private IP addresses to a public IP address for internet communication
Assigning IP addresses automatically via DHCP
Network Layer.answer: Mapping private IP addresses to a public IP address for internet communication

Network Layer.question: What is the class C IP address range?
What is hierarchical routing and why is it used?
options: Routing using only one level of routers for simplicity
Organizing routers into regions/AS to scale routing tables in large networks
Routing based on IP class hierarchy
A protocol for routing within a single LAN
Network Layer.answer: Organizing routers into regions/AS to scale routing tables in large networks

Network Layer.question: What is an interconnecting device that operates at the Network Layer?
Network Layer.answer: Router

Network Layer.question: What is the loopback address in IPv4?
What is ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) used for?
options: Resolving domain names to IP addresses
Resolving IP addresses to MAC addresses on a local network
Network Layer.answer: Resolving IP addresses to MAC addresses on a local network

Network Layer.question: What is OSPF and what algorithm does it use?
options: Open Shortest Path First; uses Dijkstra's link-state algorithm
Open Source Packet Forwarding; uses Bellman-Ford
Optimal Subnet Path Finding; uses distance vector
Overloaded Shared Path Framework; uses spanning tree
Network Layer.answer: Open Shortest Path First; uses Dijkstra's link-state algorithm

Network Layer.question: What is the subnet mask for a Class C network?
What is the BGP routing protocol used for?
options: Routing between autonomous systems on the internet
Network Layer.answer: Routing between autonomous systems on the internet

Network Layer.question: What is IP fragmentation?
options: Breaking large IP packets into smaller fragments to fit the MTU of a link
Splitting TCP segments at the IP level
Network Layer.answer: Breaking large IP packets into smaller fragments to fit the MTU of a link

Network Layer.question: What is DHCP used for?
options: Automatically assigning IP addresses and network configuration to devices
Network Layer.answer: Automatically assigning IP addresses and network configuration to devices

Network Layer.question: What is a broadcast address in a subnet?
options: The first address in a subnet
The last address in a subnet, used to send packets to all hosts in the subnet
Network Layer.answer: The last address in a subnet, used to send packets to all hosts in the subnet

Network Layer.question: What is the count-to-infinity problem in distance vector routing?
options: The hop count limit causing slow convergence when routing loops form
Unlimited number of routers in a network
A mathematical overflow in routing calculations
Network Layer.answer: The hop count limit causing slow convergence when routing loops form

Network Layer.question: What is the private IP address range for Class B?
What does a default gateway do in network routing?
options: Forwards packets destined for addresses outside the local subnet
Provides DNS services to the network
Network Layer.answer: Forwards packets destined for addresses outside the local subnet

Network Layer.question: What is the total number of IPv4 addresses?
Network Layer.answer: 2^32 ≈ 4.3 billion

Network Layer.question: How many host bits does a /28 subnet have?
What is the purpose of ICMP in networking?
options: To carry user data between applications
To report errors and diagnostic information about network conditions (e.g., ping)
Network Layer.answer: To report errors and diagnostic information about network conditions (e.g., ping)

Network Layer.question: What is split horizon in distance vector routing?
options: Not advertising a route back to the interface from which it was learned, to prevent routing loops
A horizontal routing protocol for flat networks
Network Layer.answer: Not advertising a route back to the interface from which it was learned, to prevent routing loops

Data Link Layer.question: What is the primary function of the Data Link Layer?
options: Reliable node-to-node data transfer and framing between directly connected devices
Data Link Layer.answer: Reliable node-to-node data transfer and framing between directly connected devices

Data Link Layer.question: What is a MAC address?
options: A 48-bit hardware address permanently assigned to a network interface card
Data Link Layer.answer: A 48-bit hardware address permanently assigned to a network interface card

Data Link Layer.question: What is CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) used for?
options: Error detection by appending a checksum to frames
Data Link Layer.answer: Error detection by appending a checksum to frames

Data Link Layer.question: What is the CSMA/CD protocol used for?
options: Multiple access control in Ethernet — detect and handle collisions
Secure data transmission in wired networks
Data Link Layer.answer: Multiple access control in Ethernet — detect and handle collisions

Data Link Layer.question: What is the difference between error detection and error correction?
options: Error detection finds errors; correction requires retransmission (no correction possible)
Error detection identifies errors; error correction can fix errors without retransmission
Error correction only works for single-bit errors
Data Link Layer.answer: Error detection identifies errors; error correction can fix errors without retransmission

Data Link Layer.question: What is Ethernet and at which OSI layer does it primarily operate?
options: A LAN technology operating at Layers 1 and 2 (Data Link and Physical)
An application-layer standard for file sharing
Data Link Layer.answer: A LAN technology operating at Layers 1 and 2 (Data Link and Physical)

Data Link Layer.question: What does ARP do at the Data Link Layer?
options: Resolves domain names to IP addresses
Resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses for local network communication
Data Link Layer.answer: Resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses for local network communication

Data Link Layer.question: What is a MAC (Media Access Control) protocol?
options: A protocol for managing MAC addresses
A protocol defining rules for how devices share a broadcast channel
An encryption protocol for wireless networks
A routing protocol for switched networks
Data Link Layer.answer: A protocol defining rules for how devices share a broadcast channel

Data Link Layer.question: What is the ALOHA protocol?
options: An early random access protocol where stations transmit and retransmit on collision
A Data Link error correction scheme
Data Link Layer.answer: An early random access protocol where stations transmit and retransmit on collision

Data Link Layer.question: What is CSMA/CA used for and where?
options: Collision avoidance in wireless networks (WiFi) where detection is not possible
Collision avoidance in fiber optic networks
A wired Ethernet improvement over CSMA/CD
Data Link Layer.answer: Collision avoidance in wireless networks (WiFi) where detection is not possible

Data Link Layer.question: What is the structure of an Ethernet frame?
options: Preamble, Destination MAC, Source MAC, EtherType/Length, Data, FCS
Only source and destination addresses with data
Data Link Layer.answer: Preamble, Destination MAC, Source MAC, EtherType/Length, Data, FCS

Data Link Layer.question: What does a switch use to forward frames to the correct port?
options: MAC address table (CAM table) mapping MAC addresses to ports
Data Link Layer.answer: MAC address table (CAM table) mapping MAC addresses to ports

Data Link Layer.question: What is a broadcast in Ethernet?
options: Sending data to a single specific host
Sending a frame to all devices on the local network (MAC address FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF)
Sending data to a group of selected hosts
Data Link Layer.answer: Sending a frame to all devices on the local network (MAC address FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF)

Data Link Layer.question: What is the purpose of the FCS (Frame Check Sequence) in Ethernet?
options: Contains CRC value for error detection at the receiver
Marks the start of the frame
Data Link Layer.answer: Contains CRC value for error detection at the receiver

Data Link Layer.question: What is a VLAN (Virtual LAN)?
options: A logical grouping of devices on different physical segments as if on the same LAN
A virtual private network for remote access
Data Link Layer.answer: A logical grouping of devices on different physical segments as if on the same LAN

Data Link Layer.question: What is the Hamming code used for?
options: Encrypting data at the Data Link Layer
Data Link Layer.answer: Single-bit error detection and correction

Data Link Layer.question: What is slotted ALOHA and how does it improve pure ALOHA?
options: Slotted ALOHA is identical to pure ALOHA
Slotted ALOHA synchronizes transmissions to time slots, improving efficiency from 18% to 37%
Slotted ALOHA reduces bandwidth by half
Data Link Layer.answer: Slotted ALOHA synchronizes transmissions to time slots, improving efficiency from 18% to 37%

Data Link Layer.question: What is the purpose of a preamble in an Ethernet frame?
options: Synchronizes the receiver's clock to the incoming bit stream
Marks the end of the frame
Data Link Layer.answer: Synchronizes the receiver's clock to the incoming bit stream

Data Link Layer.question: What is binary exponential backoff in CSMA/CD?
options: A method to increase data rate after each collision
After a collision, each station waits a random time from an exponentially growing range before retrying
Backing off from the network permanently after multiple collisions
Exponentially decreasing the frame size after collisions
Data Link Layer.answer: After a collision, each station waits a random time from an exponentially growing range before retrying

Data Link Layer.question: What does a bridge do in networking?
options: Connects different physical network segments at the Data Link Layer, filtering traffic by MAC address
Routes packets between different IP networks
Connects wireless and wired networks only
Amplifies signals for longer transmission distances
Data Link Layer.answer: Connects different physical network segments at the Data Link Layer, filtering traffic by MAC address

Data Link Layer.question: What is the minimum frame size in Ethernet and why?
options: 64 bytes; to detect collisions before transmission ends in CSMA/CD
Data Link Layer.answer: 64 bytes; to detect collisions before transmission ends in CSMA/CD

Data Link Layer.question: What is the STP (Spanning Tree Protocol) used for?
options: Preventing loops in bridged Ethernet networks by creating a loop-free logical topology
Splitting large networks into tree structures
A routing protocol for tree-topology networks
Data Link Layer.answer: Preventing loops in bridged Ethernet networks by creating a loop-free logical topology

Data Link Layer.question: In TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access), how do stations share the channel?
options: Each station is assigned a specific time slot for transmission
By random access with collision detection
Data Link Layer.answer: Each station is assigned a specific time slot for transmission

Data Link Layer.question: What does the EtherType field in an Ethernet frame indicate?
options: The error type in the frame
Which network-layer protocol is encapsulated in the payload (e.g., 0x0800 for IPv4)
Data Link Layer.answer: Which network-layer protocol is encapsulated in the payload (e.g., 0x0800 for IPv4)

Data Link Layer.question: What is the difference between half-duplex and full-duplex Ethernet?
options: Half-duplex can send and receive simultaneously; full-duplex cannot
Full-duplex allows simultaneous send and receive; half-duplex is one direction at a time
Full-duplex is only for fiber optic cables
Data Link Layer.answer: Full-duplex allows simultaneous send and receive; half-duplex is one direction at a time

Data Link Layer.question: What is multicast in Ethernet networking?
options: Sending frames to a specific group of devices using a multicast MAC address
Data Link Layer.answer: Sending frames to a specific group of devices using a multicast MAC address

Data Link Layer.question: What is a collision domain, and how do switches reduce it?
options: A collision domain is a network segment; switches create separate collision domain per port, eliminating shared collisions
Switches have no effect on collision domains
Switches merge multiple collision domains into one
Data Link Layer.answer: A collision domain is a network segment; switches create separate collision domain per port, eliminating shared collisions

Data Link Layer.question: What is flow control at the Data Link Layer?
options: Managing the rate of data transmission between sender and receiver at the frame level
Data Link Layer.answer: Managing the rate of data transmission between sender and receiver at the frame level

Data Link Layer.question: What is FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access)?
options: Assigning different time slots to users
Dividing the bandwidth into frequency bands, each assigned to a different user
Data Link Layer.answer: Dividing the bandwidth into frequency bands, each assigned to a different user

Data Link Layer.question: What is the maximum payload size (MTU) of a standard Ethernet frame?
Data Link Layer.answer: 1500 bytes
